Combined two consecutive recordings that were not otherwise modified, using one second silence to separate them. Recording was made only about four months after Hurricane Idalia had demolished most of the buildings on Horseshoe Beach. Construction underway on nearly every street. Litter that was scattered for miles outside the town alerted visitors of the devastation. This recording was made on a dunes trail of a crow that was calling from near the top of a pine tree where it had a good view of recordist. No other humans were nearby. Interesting variation in number of caws in a series. After the 1 second silence, there are two unusual two-syllabled caw-aws (at 6:08) which the crow made when I took a white notecard out of my pocket. There was also a long pause (almost 30 seconds) when I reversed direction on the trail near the end of the second recording (at 7:32). Temp. was 37 degrees F., cloudy becoming sunny. An armadillo was foraging nearby on the trail on my way back to the car.

Navasota River bottoms in hardword forest.

There is nothing remarkable about mobbing crows, but in this case from the moment I was aware of them, about 10:00 until dafter dusk, about 19:30, a mob of crows, at times including one or more fish crows, mobbed an unseen predator continuously throughout the entire day. The following day there was no mobbing leading me to suspect that predator, high in a pine tree, was likely a Great Horned Owl, which departed after dark.
recorded at NFC station

Observed two American crows interacting in plum tree approximately 15 feet tall. Vocalizing bird held stick in bill, vocalized as recorded, as a second bird looked on. Considering time of year, plausibly a courtship interaction was observed, including this recorded vocalization. Amplified, high pass filter. Recorded with Olympus LS-P4 Linear PCM Recorder.
Calls with hoarse voices from a group of 35 individuals perched in four or five nearby tall evergreen trees on eastern side of Swan Lake.
Six birds mobbing a large unseen raptor. Barred Owl (Strix varia) is consistently found here roosting in nearby Red Cedar (Juniperus virginiana).

A murder of crows gathered in my neighbor's field and began calling. 45 degrees, sunny, light breeze. We hear crows often but what was unique about today was that one crow in particular was enjoying him/her-self by imitating a barred owl. This mimicry went on for over an hour. Topography: A running creek flanked by trees meanders thru hills and valleys with mixed open fields and mature tree stands. Recording was trimmed, amplified, and a fade in/out was added. Residential neighbourhood, an hour before sunset. Huge (50+) assembly of crows flying and gathering in the tops of trees in this neighbourhood, making quite a racket.  From context, I guess these are alarm calls.  

At about 00:15 you first hear a raven (lower pitched, more guttural sound), who has just flown into range.  Raven is being chased by two crows, who swoop at him; the crows seem to want to drive off the raven; a third crow join the fight.  The raven rolls and dives out of the way, calling out.  They fly out of sight.
A small gang, 3- 5 birds, mobbing an unseen predator.  Likely a Barred Owl or a Cooper's Hawk.  A raucus sound that carries long distances.

This  raspy call is probably best described as a predator alert call to bring other crows in for defense.  In this case there were four birds.  One in one tree, three in another.  The three may well have been juvenile birds.  There were no predators in view.  Crows can be heard a great distances mobbing species such as Great Horned Owl.

Trying to capture all the vocalizations of crows might fill a full page of XC.  This one, in my garden, used a precise four call sequence for over two minutes.  The intervals between the series of calls varied only slightly.  There is only a little noise reduction applied here to reduce the hiss of the AudioMoth, and normalization to -09.

The most interesting part of the cut may also be the most difficult to hear.  Toward the end of the cut, one of the crows appears to be taking gulps of air and then hunching its back with its bill tucked down into its chest while making soft *hoo-woo* sounds @ approx 600Hz.  There are 3 of these sounds: at 1:52, 1:57, and 2:03.

Also includes a rattle call toward the end.

natural vocalizations; calls from an adult bird that was part of a larger flock, in coniferous forest at the river mouth. The crows in this area of Washington are somewhat hard to place taxonomically, but in this area are generally considered to be closer to Northwestern Crow than American Crow.

natural vocalizations; calls from a flock of birds moving around a pile of driftwood along a sandy beach.  The crows in this area have a murky taxonomy, but it seems generally accepted that the ones in this area are American Crows rather than Northwestern Crow.

"Glurp" calls given from single bird perched on the edge of a rooftop (no other crows noted in the immediate vicinity).  Each call was accompanied by a downward head movement, and some of the calls include a sharp clicking sound that may be bill-snapping, though I couldn't see the bill well at this point in the calls since the bill was thrust sharply downward towards the breast and obscured from my viewpoint.   "Caw" calls given later from the same individual as it flew off from the rooftop and perched in a nearby tree.

Midnight calls from an urban roosting flock of perhaps 20000 birds in the trees around my house.  An amazing experience.  Entire trees dripping with crows.  Periodically a tree would explode into a swirling crow vortex. Significantly more nasal and high-pitched than normal daytime calls. A poor quality video of just part of the flock: <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gi1Q-fufM8M>

Quite a bit of noise from planes and cars passing by.  Filtered the bottom 500Hz fairly heavily.

Calls from an urban roosting flock of  perhaps 20000 in the trees around my house.  Not the greatest recording, but a fantastic experience.  Entire trees dripping with crows.  Most of the birds are roosting quietly, some squabbling, complaining, and re-locating. Calls seem quite a bit higher-pitched than normal daytime calls. A poor quality video of just part of the flock: <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gi1Q-fufM8M>

Lots of noise from planes and cars passing by.  Filtered the bottom 500Hz fairly heavily.
Vocalizations from large flock staging in shoreline trees before flight out to nighttime roost site on nearby islands.
